Sincere Christians rejoice in Christ Jesus. The prophet calls the false prophets dumb dogs; this is what the apostle seems to refer to. Dogs, because of their malice against those who faithfully profess the gospel of Christ, bark at them and bite them. They urged human works in opposition to faith in Christ, but Paul calls them evil-workers. He calls them the concision, as they tore apart the church of Christ and cut it to pieces. Religious work is pointless unless the heart is involved; we must worship God with the strength and grace of the Holy Spirit. They rejoice in Christ Jesus, not in mere outward enjoyment and performance. We must also be careful to guard against those who oppose or abuse the doctrine of free salvation. If the apostle had wanted to boast and trust in his own flesh, he would have had as much reason as anyone. But what he considered gain while a Pharisee, what he had counted up, he considered loss for Christ. The apostle did not persuade them to do anything that he himself did not; nor did he encourage them to risk anything beyond what he risked with his never-dying soul. He regarded all these things as nothing compared to the knowledge of Christ through faith in his person and salvation. He speaks of all worldly pleasures and outward privileges which sought a place in his heart, or could pretend to any merit, and considered them loss; but someone might say, 'It is easy to say so; what would he do when he faced a trial?' He had suffered the loss of everything for the privileges of being a Christian. No, he not only considered these things as loss, but as worthless refuse, scraps thrown to dogs—not just less valuable than Christ, but utterly contemptible when set up in opposition to him. True knowledge of Christ changes and transforms people, their judgments and behavior, making them new creations. The believer prefers Christ, knowing that it is better for us to be without all worldly riches than without Christ and his word. Let us see what the apostle resolved to hold onto—Christ and heaven. We are undone without righteousness in which to appear before God, because we are guilty. There is a righteousness provided for us in Jesus Christ, and it is a complete and perfect righteousness. No one can benefit from it who trusts in themselves. Faith is the appointed way of applying the saving benefits. It is through faith in Christ’s blood. We become like Christ in his death when we die to sin, as he died for sin; and the world is crucified to us, and we to the world, by the cross of Christ. The apostle was willing to do or suffer anything to attain the glorious resurrection of saints. This hope and prospect carried him through all difficulties in his work. He did not hope to attain it through his own merit and righteousness, but through the merit and righteousness of Jesus Christ.

This simple dependence and earnestness of soul were not mentioned as if the apostle had gained the prize or were already made perfect in the Savior’s likeness. He forgot the things that were behind so as not to be content with past labors or present measures of grace. He reached forward, stretched himself toward his goal; expressions showing great concern to become more and more like Christ. Whoever runs a race must never stop short of the end but press forward as fast as he can; so those who have heaven in view must still press forward to it, with holy desires and hopes, and constant efforts. Eternal life is the gift of God, but it is in Christ Jesus; through his hand it must come to us, as it is procured for us by him. There is no getting to heaven as our home except through Christ as our Way. True believers, in seeking this assurance, as well as to glorify him, will seek more closely to resemble his sufferings and death by dying to sin and by crucifying the flesh with its affections and lusts. In these things there is a great difference among real Christians, but all know something of them. Believers make Christ everything, and set their hearts on another world. If they differ from one another and are not of the same judgment in lesser matters, yet they must not judge one another; while they all meet now in Christ and hope to meet shortly in heaven. Let them join in all the great things in which they are agreed and wait for further light as to lesser things wherein they differ. The enemies of the cross of Christ care only about their sensual appetites. Sin is the sinner’s shame, especially when it is boasted in. The way of those who care about earthly things may seem pleasant, but death and hell are at the end of it. If we choose their way, we will share their end. The life of a Christian is in heaven, where his Head and his home are, and where he hopes to be shortly; he sets his affections on things above; and where his heart is, there will his conversation be. There is glory kept for the bodies of the saints, in which they will appear at the resurrection. Then the body will be made glorious—not only raised again to life but raised to great advantage. Observe the power by which this change will be brought about. May we always be prepared for the coming of our Judge; looking to have our vile bodies changed by his Almighty power, and applying to him daily to new-create our souls unto holiness; to deliver us from our enemies, and to employ our bodies and souls as instruments of righteousness in his service.
